_meta.description: PETERMAYER case study library. Each entry is a structured record the email generation filter can match against prospect context.

case_studies.when: The WON'T BOW DOWN campaign launched around 2019. Frame this as work from a few years back, not as recent or just-published.
case_studies.situation: New Orleans is a football town. The Pelicans were the less-beloved kid brother to the Saints. Thin crowds led to fewer advertisers, less TV coverage, and a paradise for ticket scalpers. PETERMAYER's brief was to make the team as valuable to the community as its NFL counterpart.
case_studies.strategic_move: The Pelicans' problem was not the team. It was the relationship between the team and the city. We did not try to make people love the team. We made loving the team a way of loving the city. The cultural insight: defiance is the city's defining trait. Disrespect New Orleans and even our biggest naysayers come after you. Civic pride became the route to fan affinity.
case_studies.work: WON'T BOW DOWN, a campaign built on a lyric from an old Mardi Gras Indian song that New Orleanians instantly recognized. The launch teased the meaning before revealing it. Mysterious 'WBD' letters appeared everywhere from player hats to bat signal projections, getting the city guessing. The reveal landed right before the season. The campaign extended into the locker room itself and into anthemic murals throughout the city.
case_studies.results: 78M organic reach. 12,000 season tickets sold (filling two-thirds of Smoothie King Arena after a 33-49 season). 424K interactions. 360K likes in weeks. Coverage from regional media and the rest of the NBA. Tripadvisor and iSpot praise.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Sports teams of any level. Regional brands fighting for civic mindshare. Brands with an awareness or affinity gap. Underdog brands. Any brand that would benefit from being reframed as a civic icon rather than a commercial product. Brands looking to draft off cultural capital they don't currently own. Less obviously: visitor attractions wanting to convert from tourist destination to local civic asset, financial brands looking to anchor in regional identity, university or institutional brands where the audience is emotional but the brand is institutional. Live sports or live entertainment properties (leagues, arenas, touring shows) where the challenge is building fan affinity or civic/cultural connection should reach for Pelicans even when the prospect has a streaming or media component. The civic-pride-as-route-to-fan-affinity playbook is a stronger match than any embodied-vs-mediated case for live-event brands.
case_studies.relative_positioning: Pick Pelicans when the prospect's pain is fan affinity, civic connection, or the gap between being known and being loved. Strongest match for sports teams, regional live-event properties, and brands whose growth requires becoming culturally synonymous with the place they operate. Prefer Pelicans over Kennedy for live sports and live entertainment even when there is a media component — the civic-pride play travels better than the embodied-vs-screens play for live-event brands.

case_studies.when: We worked with Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex from around 2015 through last year, a longstanding relationship. The LOOK UP campaign itself comes from the first half of that engagement (roughly 2015-2019). Frame as long-running client work whose creative thinking has continued to inform the firm. Do not call LOOK UP recent.
case_studies.situation: With the Space Shuttle grounded, Kennedy Space Center went from a launchpad to another Florida attraction overnight. The Visitor Complex needed to make space travel as compelling as Disney World and Universal Studios.
case_studies.strategic_move: Kennedy's problem was not that people had stopped caring about space. It was that they had started experiencing space through screens. The Visitor Complex was selling proximity to a thing people thought they had already consumed. Our reframe: you can watch a launch on YouTube, but you cannot feel the ground shake. The campaign hinges on the difference between mediated experience and embodied experience.
case_studies.work: LOOK UP, a campaign that pulled the screens away and compelled people to touch, feel, and experience space exploration firsthand. The name works on two levels: the literal physical action a visitor takes at the Complex, and a metaphorical posture about wonder.
case_studies.results: $5.7MM campaign-attributed revenue. 121MM organic impressions. 49% new visitors. Ranked the #1 Attraction in the U.S. on Tripadvisor.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Visitor attractions of any kind. Theme parks. Zoos. Aquariums. Science centers. Museums. National park gateways and visitor complexes. Family destination brands competing against entertainment alternatives. Any brand whose subject matter consumers experience through media but whose business model depends on selling the embodied version. Less obviously: live sports properties whose problem is 'people watch on TV, why come to the stadium,' retail brands where Instagram has displaced the store visit, restaurant brands competing with delivery for the dine-in occasion.
case_studies.relative_positioning: Pick Kennedy when the prospect's pain is the embodied-vs-mediated gap — the product delivers something people think they have already consumed through screens. Strongest match for museums, science centers, zoos, aquariums, and cultural institutions. For live sports and live entertainment properties, prefer Pelicans (civic pride / fan affinity) over Kennedy even when a media component is in play.
case_studies.relationship_credential: PETERMAYER worked with Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex from around 2015 through last year. A near-decade relationship whose creative thinking has continued to inform the firm.

case_studies.when: from our work a few years back
case_studies.situation: Arlington, Texas sits between Dallas and Fort Worth, home to Six Flags, the Texas Rangers, and the Dallas Cowboys. The city draws 14 million annual visitors but only half stay overnight. The rest treat Arlington as a day trip from bigger cities they assume have more diverse overnight activities. The Convention & Visitors Bureau needed Arlington to stand as a destination in its own right, not a waypoint to someplace else.
case_studies.strategic_move: We stopped fighting Dallas and Fort Worth for destination mindshare and claimed a territory they couldn't occupy. Arlington's advantage was the kind of joy its mix of attractions delivered: spontaneous, playful, childlike, the feeling of a day unfolding like a playground. The target audience — young parents deep in the demands of family life — was wistful about the freedom their own kids had. Arlington became the practical answer to that emotional need for release. Dreamier than Dallas, more fun than Fort Worth.
case_studies.work: WORLD OF WONDERFUL, a brand campaign built around the Wonderful family — animated characters with distinct interests who bond over a trip to Arlington. The campaign lived across cinema, digital streaming, social media, DFW airport OOH, and custom Spotify playlists per character. The Visit Arlington social channels did themed takeovers by each Wonderful family member. The tagline "Welcome to Arlington, a world of wonderful" doubled as differentiator and mantra.
case_studies.results: 11% lift in overnight visitation year-over-year. 32% lift in room revenue year-over-year. 26% increase in organic site traffic. 25% growth in social media followers.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Destination marketing organizations, CVBs, and regional tourism boards. Mid-sized cities or regions that have strong assets but are overshadowed by larger neighbors. Any destination brand with a category miscategorization problem — it has the product, but consumers are slotting it as something less ambitious than it is. Family travel and attractions brands where the creative challenge is emotional release rather than thrill-claim. Less obviously, any consumer brand sitting in the shadow of a larger category leader where the win requires claiming a different emotional territory rather than competing on the same one.
case_studies.relative_positioning: Pick Arlington over other destination cases when the prospect's situation involves a larger competitor they can't outspend or outshine, and the strategic move needs to be claiming emotional territory the competitor can't. Strongest case when the prospect is a mid-sized city, regional attraction, or category-shadowed brand.

case_studies.when: from PETERMAYER's work about a decade ago
case_studies.situation: Asheville draws travelers to Biltmore Mansion and outdoor activities, but the region was experiencing stagnant growth. Visitors from nearby geographies were coming for those two things and leaving, missing the broader range of experiences the city offers. The Convention & Visitors Bureau needed to compel deeper exploration without abandoning what was already drawing people in.
case_studies.strategic_move: The experiential traveler segment had quietly shifted. What the category was still selling — escape from daily life — had been replaced in this audience's actual mindset with something different: discovery. Both inward (who am I becoming through this trip) and outward (what am I finding that my friends haven't). Most competing destinations were still positioning around escape. Asheville could own discovery, a territory the category hadn't adjusted to. That reframe drove the recommendation of a younger cohort target and became the entire campaign platform.
case_studies.work: DISCOVERY, INSIDE & OUT. A 360-degree campaign built on the stories experiential travelers tell each other about treasured finds, from mountain vistas to downtown eclectic energy and everything in between. OOH lines like "Traffic's not our jam," "Break out of the perimeter," and "Live at your own speed" positioned Asheville as a pace-of-life antidote to bigger metros. Custom influencer content partnerships carried niche verticals (The Art of Asheville, Asheville Music Scene, Asheville's Best Outdoor Adventures, A Food Lover's Guide to Asheville) for targeted segments. Built through PETERMAYER's ALL IN brand-building process, which surfaced the core brand statement.
case_studies.results: $1.9 billion in direct visitor spending. 26% increase in overnight visitation. 91% increase in lodging sales. 85%+ reach in target markets.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Destination marketing organizations, CVBs, and tourism brands, particularly those with underleveraged diversity of experience beyond one or two dominant attractions. Any category where the audience has outgrown the category's default emotional premise (escape, relaxation, status, convenience) and a more specific emotional territory is available to claim. Brands whose growth problem is audience mindset rather than product fit. Less obviously, wellness and health brands where consumers have moved beyond self-care as indulgence, retail categories where consumers have moved beyond shopping as leisure, financial services where consumers have moved beyond planning as obligation. The strategic move travels anywhere a category is still selling a yesterday motivation to an audience that has moved on.
case_studies.relative_positioning: Pick Asheville over Petoskey when the emotional territory is about discovery and novelty rather than reset and return. Pick Asheville over Arlington when the competitive pattern is category mindset shift rather than claiming territory from named neighbors.

case_studies.when: a longstanding partnership running from 2018 through 2023
case_studies.situation: Coastal Mississippi is a real Gulf Coast destination overshadowed by Florida and Alabama beach markets. The CVB had broad awareness problems and a perception problem compounding them — the state of Mississippi carried reputational baggage that made travelers hesitant even when the coast itself had reasons to visit. Over five years the work weathered hurricanes, an algae bloom, COVID-19, and in 2020, a social moment that required an audience-specific evolution.
case_studies.strategic_move:
Two strategic chapters in one long client relationship.

Chapter one inverted typical destination marketing. Rather than asking visitors to come and share, we positioned Coastal Mississippi as The Secret Coast and told audiences NOT to visit and ruin the surprises. We turned typical DMO strategy on its head by asking visitors not to share the destination so they could keep it as their own. Counterintuitive. Talk-value. Increased year-over-year demand even through external crises.

Chapter two adapted the Secret Coast platform for a perception gap we identified through qualitative research. Multicultural travelers — mostly Black and Latino — were finding their way to Coastal Mississippi and finding authentic welcome, vibrant diversity, knowledgeable local guides. But prospective visitors who hadn't been there carried blanket apprehension about the state of Mississippi itself. The strategic move: instead of speaking to these audiences, we shared the creation of the campaign with people who represented them. Minority-owned businesses and attractions were featured alongside reliable draws. Local talent starred in and captured the work. The campaign was built with the audiences it aimed to reach.
case_studies.work:
THE SECRET COAST platform, across both chapters.

Chapter one's creative told travelers specifically not to share, built around the nervous internal dialogue of posting vacation photos and risking your secret spot getting overrun. Lines like "You'll find the best fishing right here in [REDACTED]. Discover our best-kept secrets." The "Delete Post?" visual device carried through into digital display ads with lines like "Plan your trip. Spare the details" and "Sunscreen on. Location services off."

Chapter two extended the same device with two video spots ("Sister" and "Grandparents") that took viewers into the inner conflict of posting vacation photos and imagining the wave of friends and relatives who would descend on the secret. In the moment of clarity, they decide not to post. The secret coast stays secret a little longer. The spots featured local diverse talent throughout and drew on minority-owned businesses across the 13 towns that comprise the Coast.
case_studies.results: Through the Secret Coast platform, year-over-year demand increased through hurricanes, an algae bloom, and COVID-19. Post-pandemic numbers: 17% increase in destination revenue from Q3 2019 to Q3 2022. 22% increase in average daily hotel rate between 2019 and 2022. 33% increase in hotel revenue over 2019.
case_studies.use_for_narrative:
Two distinct prospect situations.

Chapter one travels to any destination or brand whose authenticity depends on being under-the-radar, any category where everyone knows about it reduces the appeal, brands that would benefit from counterintuitive positioning that rewards in-group knowledge, tourism brands competing against louder neighbors where volume marketing is a losing strategy. Also travels to consumer brands in insider-driven categories (specialty food, craft beverages, indie retail, independent media) where growth is about finding the right audience rather than the biggest audience.

Chapter two travels to destination marketing organizations, tourism boards, or consumer brands facing a perception gap with multicultural audiences, particularly in geographies or categories where demographic assumptions create barriers to visitation or consideration. Any brand where the answer isn't tell these audiences about us but make the work with these audiences. Less obviously, any brand whose audience has moved on from the brand's default understanding of them — healthcare brands speaking to audiences whose care patterns have shifted, financial services brands speaking to generational wealth transfer, education brands whose traditional audience has changed.
case_studies.relative_positioning: When choosing between Coastal Mississippi chapter one and Arlington, Arlington is about claiming territory from named competitors (Dallas, Fort Worth) while Coastal Mississippi is about inverting the category's sharing-and-discovery dynamic entirely. Use Coastal Mississippi when the counterintuitive play is the point. For chapter two, no other case study in the library covers the multicultural perception gap pattern — use it alone when that situation applies.
case_studies.relationship_credential: PETERMAYER was Coastal Mississippi's creative partner for five years through hurricanes, an algae bloom, COVID-19, and a social moment that required us to evolve the platform rather than abandon it. Longstanding client relationships that adapt through crisis are themselves a credential.

case_studies.client: St. Augustine, Ponte Vedra & The Beaches
case_studies.when: from our archive, foundational work that has continued to inform our thinking
case_studies.situation: St. Augustine, Ponte Vedra & The Beaches is America's oldest settlement, with 450 years of history, historic architecture, deep cultural roots, and 42 miles of coastline. But inside a state known nationally for sunshine and beaches, the destination kept getting lost. Visitation skewed heavily to Florida-based Baby Boomer couples. Only 20% of visitors were Millennials, despite clear growth potential with a younger, more sophisticated traveler. The destination needed a platform that captured what made it distinct without abandoning its core audience.
case_studies.strategic_move: We identified the audience we were actually competing for, the Purpose Pursuer — a Millennial and Boomer psychographic that wanted meaningful, immersive travel that let them grow and recharge rather than just sunburn and come home. When we looked at their behavior versus their aspirations, we saw the gap that defined the opportunity. They loved the idea of international travel but their actual travel behavior was shorter, more affordable, and domestic. The strategic insight: St. Augustine already delivered what this audience wanted from international travel — old world charm, cultural density, 450 years of history, a sense of being transported somewhere else — without the cost, stress, or burden of leaving the country. We named the gap and claimed the territory. Traveling Intranationally.
case_studies.work: TRAVELING INTRANATIONALLY, a brand platform built on the insight that St. Augustine could deliver the emotional payoff of international travel in a domestic package. The creative brought to life the feeling that a visit transported travelers somewhere faraway without ever leaving home, building on the destination's distinctive tapestry of heritage and cultural blend. The platform gave the destination an ownable creative idea that differentiated it from every other Florida beach play and appealed to audiences who wanted travel to mean something beyond a week of sun.
case_studies.results: 5% increase in visitation. 7.5% increase in revenue. 7.5% increase in room nights booked. 6.4% increase in website visitation.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Travel and tourism brands, particularly those with distinctive heritage or cultural depth that gets overshadowed by higher-volume category competitors. Destinations with the raw material for a differentiated positioning but stuck in a category default. Most valuably, though, this case study applies to any brand whose audience has a gap between aspiration and actual behavior — where consumers want one thing but their behavior points somewhere else. The strategic move is to identify the gap, name it, and claim the territory where your product delivers the aspirational emotional payoff through the accessible product. Examples: a wellness brand whose audience aspires to health retreats but actually just wants a walk in the morning; a financial services brand whose audience aspires to a wealth advisor but behaves like a Robinhood user; an education brand whose audience aspires to graduate degrees but actually watches YouTube tutorials; a food brand whose audience aspires to fine dining but orders delivery; a fitness brand whose audience aspires to personal training but works out at home. Any category where the aspiration-behavior gap is the strategic territory available to claim.
case_studies.relative_positioning: St. Augustine is the most broadly portable case in the library. Use it whenever the prospect has a clear aspiration-behavior gap that their current positioning doesn't name. Prefer it over Arlington and Asheville when the prospect problem is about audience pattern rather than competitive or mindset patterns.

case_studies.when: ongoing client engagement from 2023 through 2025, current active work
case_studies.situation: Delaware North Parks & Resorts runs 14 properties across the country, but the portfolio operated like 14 separate businesses. Different audiences, different seasons, different revenue pressures. Budgets ranged from high six figures to "don't waste a dollar." No unified performance system meant no way to compare impact, track ROAS, or shift spend to where it actually worked. When PETERMAYER came in, media was dark, the fiscal clock was ticking, and the mandate was clear: turn performance on and make every dollar prove it belongs.
case_studies.strategic_move: Unlike the rest of this library, this case study is about operational discipline, not brand positioning. The move was rejecting the one-size-fits-all impulse that fragments performance across portfolios, and rejecting the opposite impulse to let each property run in total isolation with no comparable measurement. We built one disciplined performance system with local inputs, centralized measurement, and the authority to shift money fast. Fourteen distinct media plans, each matched to a property's realities and revenue pressure. One framework underneath them for comparing impact and redeploying spend. The first round of data showed the early mix wouldn't hit revenue pace, so we made a controlled decisive shift: cut CTV, contextual display, and high-cost endemic partners that weren't converting. Redeployed that spend into three performance engines already proving out. No budget increase. No guesswork. No let's-give-it-time.
case_studies.work: A 14-property performance program built in six weeks with media dark. For each property we went deep on GA data, past performance, booking windows, origin markets, and syndicated research before building the plan. Creative plus-ups matched to specific properties extended the reach beyond performance channels: a Times Square OOH takeover with a branded mist-blowing double-decker bus for Niagara Falls State Park, a 4-page cover wrap of Travel + Leisure magazine hyper-targeted to affluent couples in Colorado for Camp Hale's wedding venue. The system stayed agile — performance data drove reallocation through 2024 and into 2025, with consistent year-over-year gains in attributable revenue and booking efficiency.
case_studies.results: Within two quarters, 30% ROAS lift and waste eliminated through reallocation. By Q1 2025, $5.24 ROAS representing 90% quarter-over-quarter growth, 50% increase in total attributed revenue, 2,000+ bookings from trackable media, and $126 average cost per booking across fully trackable properties.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: This case study serves a different prospect pattern than the rest of the library. Use it when the prospect's pain is performance, not positioning. Multi-location or multi-property brands with fragmented media and no unified measurement. Portfolios where corporate needs rollup clarity while individual properties need localized accountability. CMOs who inherited a media mess and need to prove every dollar. Brands constrained by flat or shrinking budgets where growth has to come from reallocation rather than incremental spend. Prospects who describe their problem in ROAS, CAC, attribution, booking-cost, or conversion terms rather than brand-affinity terms. Less obvious applications: franchise brands, hotel chains, healthcare systems with multiple facilities, regional retail chains, B2B brands with multi-vertical portfolios. Any prospect whose marketing function spans a portfolio of operating units that need to be measured consistently but activated locally.
case_studies.relative_positioning: DNPR is the only performance-marketing case in the library. If the prospect's pain is measurable business outcomes (ROAS, CAC, attribution), use DNPR. If the prospect's pain is brand resonance or creative differentiation, do not reach for DNPR — use a brand positioning case instead. The two archetypes do not substitute for each other.
case_studies.email_usage_note: The connective tissue in emails works differently for this case study. Instead of "we saw a parallel pattern when we worked with X" (insight parallel), use framings like "we built a performance system for DNPR that turned 14 fragmented property budgets into a disciplined engine, and the shape of that approach applies to any multi-location brand with real revenue pressure" (process parallel). The case study is a credential for agency operational discipline, not a creative insight parallel. Do not try to stretch DNPR into a brand-positioning parallel.

case_studies.url_note: URL pending — campaign launched late 2026, add when Petoskey Area VB publishes case study
case_studies.when: fresh creative work we just put in market, campaign launched late 2026
case_studies.situation: The Petoskey Area on Lake Michigan's coast draws visitors who return year after year, but they struggle to articulate why. The destination has raw beauty (ancient stones, eternal sunsets, starry nights), character, vistas, and full amenities. But most destination marketing answers what do I do there, and that's not what Petoskey travelers were responding to. Our job was to capture the magical part of the destination that made people return and make that intangible pull tangible enough to invite more people in.
case_studies.strategic_move: We rejected the category default of destination-as-escape and found a sharper emotional role the Petoskey Area could play in people's lives. Not escape, which implies running from something. Reconnection, which implies returning to something. The destination's elemental beauty — the ancient stones, the sunsets, the starry nights — helps visitors shed anxiety and distraction and return to center. Your inner compass resets. That's a specific emotional territory most destination marketing doesn't claim, because most destinations compete on doing and seeing rather than on being and recovering. The move was giving an underfelt feeling a name and building a brand platform around it.
case_studies.work: IT'S KINDA UNREAL, a fully reimagined brand identity and campaign that lets the destination's magic speak for itself. Built across video, print, digital, and out-of-home as a coherent brand world rooted in the area's elemental beauty. The creative leaves people with more than a checklist of things to do. It leaves them with a feeling to take home: restored, recharged, and a little more like themselves again. The campaign name captures what travelers already feel when they're there but haven't been able to articulate — the you-know-what-I-mean play that turns a private feeling into a shared one.
case_studies.results: The campaign launched in late 2026. Early. No attributable performance data yet. Describe as active in-market creative whose strategic thinking is current, not as work with proven numerical outcomes.
case_studies.use_for_narrative: Destination marketing brands specifically, and more broadly any brand whose product delivers an emotional reset or recovery that the audience values but struggles to name. Wellness brands positioned around reconnection rather than escape. Outdoor and nature brands where the emotional payoff is inward rather than outward. Sleep brands, meditation brands, therapy and mental-health brands. Any consumer category where the purchase is driven less by "what will this give me" and more by "what will this let me set down." The strategic move also travels to luxury categories where the product's emotional payoff is about feeling more like yourself again, not more impressive to others.
case_studies.relative_positioning: Pick Petoskey over Asheville when the prospect's emotional territory is about reset and return rather than discovery and novelty. Pick Petoskey over St. Augustine when the aspiration-behavior gap framework doesn't apply. Pick Petoskey when you need a fresh, current case study to pair with an established older one — showing PETERMAYER does recent work, not just archive work.
case_studies.email_usage_note: Because Petoskey has no performance numbers yet, do not attempt to cite results. Cite the strategic thinking, the campaign name, and the work itself. Descriptive phrasing only. If a first-touch email asks the case study to carry proof-point weight, pair it with an older case study that has defensible numbers, or use a different case study entirely. The right pairing move: lead with Petoskey for the fresh strategic thinking, support with an older case like Asheville for the numerical proof that PETERMAYER's destination work performs.
